text: The video shows the presence of a percutaneous transhepatic cholangiography (PTC) catheter placed in the patient prior to the current operation. The surgical field reveals adhesions being addressed at the start of the procedure.
Five days post-laparoscopic cystectomy, the surgeon carefully dissects adhesions between the transverse colon and the gallbladder fossa.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the biloma collection in the subhepatic fossa, ensuring no injury occurs to the adjacent transverse colon.
A surgical clip is visible at the entrance of the dry hepatic duct, marking the point of purification.
The surgeon removes the previously placed PTC to facilitate the cephalad mobilization of the liver, aiming to expose the porta hepatis.
The surgeon dissects the falciform ligament up to the hepatic oval confluence, using a laparoscopic liver retractor to elevate the liver cephalad, enhancing visibility in the surgical area.
The surgeon lowers the Haller plate to adjust the anatomy, revealing the leaking right hepatic duct.
The surgeon resects tissue with a Bovie electrocautery, revealing the lumen of the right hepatic duct, which is being cannulated with a catheter.
The surgeon uses a cholangiogram to confirm the anatomical structures before proceeding to create the Roux limb.

The surgeon identifies the ligament of Treitz and measures approximately thirty-five centimeters distally. A mesenteric window is created, followed by the formation of a stapled side-to-side jejunojejunostomy.
The surgeon splits the omentum while ensuring the Roux limb remains vascularized during a robotic repair for bile duct injury.

The surgeon measures approximately six centimeters of the Roux limb and proceeds to create a stapled side-to-side jejunojejunostomy. A 3-0 V-Loc suture is used to secure the anastomosis, with openings created on both sides of the jejunum for the anastomosis.
The surgeon uses a robotic blue load stapler to create the anastomosis, followed by closing the common enterotomy with a running suture.
The surgeon demonstrates robotic suturing techniques to fellows, placing an anchoring stitch at the inferior corner of the common enterotomy.
The surgeon uses the robotic system to imbricate the edge of the bowel wall, enhancing the quality of the anastomosis. A new stitch is started from the opposite corner, employing a similar technique.
The surgeon ensures that the purchase on the bowel wall is adequate. The two sutures meet in the middle and are tightened, completing the anastomosis.
The bedside assistant aids in transposing the Roux limb, while the surgeon creates a small enterotomy in preparation for the hepatogastrojejunostomy.
The surgeon begins the anastomosis at the nine o'clock position, placing the index stitch on the right hepatic duct while the rest of the biliary system remains intact.
The surgeon performs a high biliary anastomosis at the level of the hilar plate, distal to the bifurcation, using the robotic system to facilitate the procedure.
The surgeon places each suture bite under direct visualization, ensuring precise spacing to prevent postoperative leakage from the bowel anastomosis.
The surgeon performs the posterior aspect of the anastomosis in a running fashion, while the bedside assistant uses suction to remove leaking bile, ensuring a clear view of the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a suture starting from the nine o'clock position to create the anterior aspect of the anastomosis, forming the front wall after completing the back wall.

The surgeon uses a regular needle holder to pass each suture needle precisely with the aid of the robotic system, ensuring each bite is placed accurately. As the anastomosis nears completion, the sutures are tied securely to ensure a tight closure.

The surgeon observes no bile leakage as the anastomosis is completed. An anchoring stitch is carefully placed at the hilum plate to reduce tension, ensuring no injury to the liver's inflow structures. Another anchoring stitch is placed at the three o'clock position, suspending the Roux limb in two locations.
The surgeon uses the robotic system to place a drain through one of the port sites in the right quadrant, ensuring proper placement and function.
